summary: Obfuscated bulk email/SMS spam and phishing toolkit
details: Package self-describes as an 'Advanced email sender' with red-team/smtp keywords and ships ~45 heavily obfuscated JavaScript files under a dotfile directory (.ad/) implementing bulk SMTP send loops, SMS gateway abuse via Twilio/Telnyx/Nexmo providers, DKIM keypair generation with a DKIM-replay forwarder (RustBackend.forwardSignedEmail in x3.js), an open-redirect scanner, IMAP/Microsoft Graph contact extraction from compromised mailboxes, and a Chrome-extension webmail session-hijack helper (web-command.js → nolimit-ext). A commercial license check in x4.js POSTs a license key plus host fingerprint to a hardcoded vendor gating server, but only when the operator invokes the CLI (activate/send/sort/validate) — not at install or require time. The postinstall script is benign: on Windows it only writes a <npm-prefix>/nolimit.cmd shim that invokes node on the package's own.ad/x0.js, with no network I/O, secret reads, or privileged writes. The package does not exfiltrate the installer's data, fetch attacker-controlled code at install time, drop a backdoor, or silently relay caller-supplied data to a third party — its malicious functionality is directed at third-party email and SMS recipients (spam victims, phishing targets, compromised-mailbox contacts), which the operator who installs and runs the CLI is knowingly driving. Heavy obfuscation across every functional module (RC4 string-array + hex identifiers) is consistent with anti-analysis of an abuse tool rather than supply-chain compromise. Routing for human review: this is contraband abuse tooling that very likely violates npm acceptable-use policy and should be considered for removal on that basis, but it is not a supply-chain attack on the developer who installs it under this system's threat model.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
